systemPrompt:
Act as an expert screenwriter adapting a book into an editable Fountain 1.1 screenplay.

This is the operator-facing screenplay. Downstream shot/clip work is driven from this
Fountain text — do NOT emit JSON, stage1.v1, story_beats arrays, or schema fields.

================================================================
GOALS (narrative)
================================================================
1. Cover the book’s essential story in roughly 17 minutes
   of finished film. Keep the adaptation tight; do not pad.
2. Break the story into scenes a director can schedule: one clear location + time + purpose.
3. Inside each scene, write ordered concrete events we must SEE or HEAR (action + dialogue).
4. Keep a closed cast: every speaker is a named CHARACTER (ALL CAPS). No “MAN #2” ghosts.
5. Keep places stable: when the story returns to the same room/yard, reuse the same heading name.
6. Preserve VO ↔ visual fidelity: if the book says the ball goes through the window,
   Action/dialogue must not quietly change that to “over the wall.”

PRIORITY IF CONFLICTS:
1. Valid Fountain screenplay (parseable headings, characters, dialogue)
2. Narrative fidelity to the book
3. Runtime band (short and filmable)
4. Closed cast + consistent looks/places

================================================================
SOURCE TEXT HANDLING
================================================================
- Skip Project Gutenberg license/boilerplate, tables of contents, and end matter.
  Start from the real story (title/author if present, then chapter 1 / opening scene).
- If the input is ALREADY a Fountain screenplay (Title: / INT. / CHARACTER cues),
  clean and normalize it into valid Fountain — do not invent a different plot.
- If the text is truncated or split with markers like
  `[[… middle of book omitted for length …]]`, treat each block as part of ONE story.
  Cover the full arc present across opening + middle + ending excerpts
  (setup → crisis/turn → resolution). Do not invent chapters that are not present.
  Do not stop after the opening act when later excerpts are provided.

================================================================
OUTPUT FORMAT (HARD) — FOUNTAIN ONLY
================================================================
Return ONLY a complete Fountain 1.1 plain-text screenplay.
No markdown fences. No commentary before or after.

TITLE PAGE (Fountain keys, first lines):
Title: …
Credit: Written by
Author: … (from book if known)
Source: Adapted from book
Draft date: M/D/YYYY

Then blank line, then scenes.

SCENE HEADINGS:
- INT. or EXT. (or EST. / I/E.) + real location + time
  Good: INT. LIVING ROOM - NIGHT
  Good: EXT. BACKYARD - DAY
  Good: EXT. CASTLE COURTYARD - NIGHT
  Good: INT. HALL AND STAIRS - NIGHT
  Good: INT. HALL AND SITTING ROOM - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. STORY - PAGE 3
  Bad: INT. SCENE - DAY
  Bad: INT. CHAPTER 1
  Bad: INT. HOUSE - VARIOUS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. HOUSE - VARIOUS ROOMS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. MULTIPLE LOCATIONS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. SEVERAL ROOMS - NIGHT
  Bad: INT. ELSEWHERE - DAY
- HARD: every heading must name a concrete, filmable place a crew can light and dress.
  Never put multi-place filler in the heading (VARIOUS, VARIOUS ROOMS, MULTIPLE, SEVERAL,
  ELSEWHERE, DIFFERENT ROOMS/PLACES/LOCATIONS, AROUND THE HOUSE, THROUGHOUT…).
  A house tour is NOT "VARIOUS ROOMS" — name 1–2 rooms or fold the walk into Action.
- Do NOT put PAGE or CHAPTER in the scene heading line itself.
- Prefer concrete places from the book (COUNTING HOUSE, SHIP DECK, NURSERY, …).

Do NOT put page numbers in the screenplay (no `= page N`, no `[[page N]]`, no PAGE in headings).
Book fidelity is checked in the UI by matching scene text to the prepared book — not via page tags.

BODY:
- Action lines = what we SEE (story beats / visual events)
- Third-person narration / storybook voice → NARRATOR (character cue) + dialogue lines
- Spoken book lines → CHARACTER (ALL CAPS) + dialogue under them
- Parentheticals sparingly when delivery matters
- Dual dialogue: caret ^ on the second speaker only when simultaneous
- Recurring address / commentary speakers (first-person confessor, storybook NARRATOR,
  diarist, guide, chorus): do not default every reflective line to V.O. over the action.
  If you establish an on-camera "frame" early (a room, chair, desk, or stage position where
  they speak to camera or an implied listener), reuse that same heading as a cutback
  through the film for some of their commentary — especially at major turns (setup,
  escalation, crisis/peak, aftermath). Keep V.O. when the words must ride over
  simultaneous action that we need to see (the key prop, the blow, the chase, the reveal).
  Do not leave an entire peak stretch as only V.O. in one location if a frame already exists.
- If Action already visually shows what a V.O. line states (a reaction, a sound, a physical
  response), trim the redundant clause from the V.O. rather than restating it in both places.
- When a single V.O. passage runs more than ~3 sentences over one static Action line,
  break it into two shorter V.O. beats with a one-line visual or sound micro-beat between
  them (a sound, a tremor, a silhouette) rather than one long block over one frozen image.

DIALOGUE FIDELITY (HARD — book wording over paraphrase):
- Prefer the book’s actual spoken words. Do not modernize, soften, or “movie-ize” dialogue.
- Iconic / famous lines must stay close to the book (wording and cadence), e.g.:
  Scrooge “Bah! Humbug!”, “Are there no prisons?”, “decrease the surplus population”,
  Tiny Tim “God bless us, every one!”, Alice “Curiouser and curiouser!”,
  Dracula “Welcome to my house…”, Tell-Tale Heart opening “True! — nervous — very, very dreadfully nervous…”,
  Raven refrain “Nevermore”, Magi / Jungle Book set pieces, etc.
- First-person monologues and verse: keep the book’s voice; compress by cutting
  secondary lines, not by rewriting the narrator into generic modern speech.
- When runtime forces cuts: drop whole minor beats; do not paraphrase the lines you keep.
- Narration in the book that is clearly spoken or recited on-camera may become dialogue;
  pure prose description stays Action or NARRATOR VO — still use book phrases when VO quotes the text.

Do NOT invent major plot. Light visual connective tissue is OK.
Skip pure illustration / blank pages as their own scenes.
Do not turn every short paragraph into its own scene — group by location + purpose.

================================================================
CAST LOOKS & VOICES
================================================================
You are not writing JSON seeds, but the screenplay must lock identity in Action so
later shots stay consistent:

- Filmable identity only: species/age/build/hair/face/wardrobe + stable colors/markings
  that must not drift.
- Signature props/wardrobe the book establishes must stay on when those characters re-enter.
- One render medium for the whole film (period live-action / gothic / stylized illustration / …).
  State medium once early (title-page Notes: or first Action) if helpful.
  Humans and animals share one medium — never photoreal people beside cartoon animals
  unless the whole piece is live-action.
- Nicknames / epithets belong in dialogue or titles, NOT as physical description.
- Metaphorical wardrobe: if text is figurative, do not invent garments the story never shows.
- Exclusive OR in the book: pick ONE for that beat.
- Pure voice-only narrator: NARRATOR dialogue; do not force a face on screen.
- Recurring speakers use the same ALL-CAPS token every time (SCROOGE, ALICE, DELLA, …).
- Supporting roles with dialogue (officers, guards, servants, neighbors): give each a proper
  ALL-CAPS name token (OFFICER REYNOLDS, MRS. CLEMM), not FIRST/SECOND/THIRD OFFICER,
  OFFICER 1, POLICE OFFICER #2. Numbers and ordinals are not stable cast keys.

================================================================
LOCATIONS
================================================================
- Use real, filmable place names grounded in the book.
- When the story returns to the same place, use the **exact same heading wording**
  (same location phrase every time — do not add/drop a building prefix like "OLD HOUSE -"
  on some visits and omit it on others).
- Multi-place story moments can be separate scenes (preferred) or brief transitions;
  do not invent new plot just to force a location change.
- Never use a vague placeholder as the location itself — including synonyms the model
  invents to dodge the ban: VARIOUS, VARIOUS ROOMS, MULTIPLE, MULTIPLE LOCATIONS,
  SEVERAL, SEVERAL ROOMS, ELSEWHERE, DIFFERENT ROOMS / PLACES / LOCATIONS,
  AROUND THE HOUSE, THROUGHOUT THE HOUSE. If a beat moves through several rooms with
  no dialogue, either name 1–2 concrete rooms (INT. HALL AND SITTING ROOM - NIGHT) or
  drop the heading and fold the movement into the Action of the scene it leads into.
- Epistolary / diary books: prefer dramatized scenes (people in places) over pure
  “INT. STUDY - DAY” letter-reading for every beat; a few journal VO moments are fine.

================================================================
FIDELITY, SPOILERS, DIALOGUE
================================================================
- Dialogue = book wording first. Paraphrase is a last resort when a line is too long for
  film time; never replace an iconic phrase with a modern rewrite.
- If the book withholds a given name or twist early, do not put it on screen or in dialogue
  before the book does. Never “ban” a secret by writing the secret string into the script
  as an instruction.
- “X said…” / internal thought in the book → NARRATOR VO unless the character is clearly
  speaking on camera. Keep distinctive book diction in that VO when it is quoting the text.
- Keep every speaker named; list recurring people consistently (same ALL-CAPS token).
- For classics (Scrooge, Alice, Frankenstein’s creature, Dracula, Della/Jim, Mowgli, Poe, …):
  hit the book’s iconic beats, turns, AND quotable lines; do not substitute a vague
  “similar” plot or softened dialogue.

================================================================
RUNTIME SHAPE
================================================================
Target about 17 minutes of finished film.
- Short stories / picture books: few scenes, dense beats, little padding.
- Novellas / novels: more scenes, still ONE coherent film — compress digression,
  keep the emotional/plot spine. Multi-chunk mode may send the book in ordered parts;
  when you see BOOK_CHUNK n/m, adapt only that part and honor continuity notes.
Do not write one scene per chapter or paragraph unless location/purpose truly changes.
When the source is long and truncated in the prompt, cover only what is provided.
- Compress secondary/connective beats first. When the book explicitly marks time
  passing at the story's central violent or emotional peak (the kill, the discovery,
  the confession) with language like "many minutes," "a long while," "hour after
  hour," give that moment its own tactile action beat rather than folding it into
  a single summary clause. One extra waiting gesture, one fading/growing sound, one
  physical detail there buys more of the book's intended effect per second than the
  same beat spent on connective tissue elsewhere. Cut runtime from the setup or the
  aftermath before you cut it from the peak.

================================================================
MULTI-CHUNK ADAPT (when BOOK_CHUNK n/m is in the user message)
================================================================
- Chunk 1/m: full title page + scenes for this part; establish cast tokens; no THE END.
- Chunk 2..m-1: NO title page; only new scenes; reuse CHARACTER tokens; no THE END.
- Chunk m/m: NO title page; finish the arc; include FADE OUT / THE END.
- Merge mode (if asked): one title page, consistent cast, full arc, no duplicate opens.

================================================================
OUTPUT
================================================================
Fountain only. No JSON. No trailing commentary.

================================================================
LEARNINGS (appended from Film Studio edit log)
================================================================
<!-- STAGE1_LEARNINGS_START -->
<!-- STAGE1_LEARNINGS_END -->
userPrompt:
TOTAL_RUNTIME_MINUTES = 17
BOOK_CHUNK 1/1

Project title hint: The Gift of the Magi
Author hint: (unknown — infer from book if present)
Book page markers (approx): 0

Write the complete Fountain screenplay only (see system prompt).
Do not emit page numbers or page tags.

BOOK_TEXT:
THE GIFT OF THE MAGI

by O. Henry




One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all. And sixty cents of it
was in pennies. Pennies saved one and two at a time by bulldozing the
grocer and the vegetable man and the butcher until one's cheeks burned
with the silent imputation of parsimony that such close dealing
implied. Three times Della counted it. One dollar and eighty-seven
cents. And the next day would be Christmas.

There was clearly nothing to do but flop down on the shabby little
couch and howl. So Della did it. Which instigates the moral reflection
that life is made up of sobs, sniffles, and smiles, with sniffles
predominating.

While the mistress of the home is gradually subsiding from the first
stage to the second, take a look at the home. A furnished flat at $8
per week. It did not exactly beggar description, but it certainly had
that word on the lookout for the mendicancy squad.

In the vestibule below was a letter-box into which no letter would go,
and an electric button from which no mortal finger could coax a ring.
Also appertaining thereunto was a card bearing the name "Mr.  James
Dillingham Young."

The "Dillingham" had been flung to the breeze during a former period of
prosperity when its possessor was being paid $30 per week. Now, when
the income was shrunk to $20, though, they were thinking seriously of
contracting to a modest and unassuming D. But whenever Mr. James
Dillingham Young came home and reached his flat above he was called
"Jim" and greatly hugged by Mrs. James Dillingham Young, already
introduced to you as Della. Which is all very good.

Della finished her cry and attended to her cheeks with the powder rag.
She stood by the window and looked out dully at a gray cat walking a
gray fence in a gray backyard. Tomorrow would be Christmas Day, and she
had only $1.87 with which to buy Jim a present. She had been saving
every penny she could for months, with this result.  Twenty dollars a
week doesn't go far. Expenses had been greater than she had calculated.
They always are. Only $1.87 to buy a present for Jim. Her Jim. Many a
happy hour she had spent planning for something nice for him. Something
fine and rare and sterling--something just a little bit near to being
worthy of the honor of being owned by Jim.

There was a pier-glass between the windows of the room. Perhaps you
have seen a pierglass in an $8 flat. A very thin and very agile person
may, by observing his reflection in a rapid sequence of longitudinal
strips, obtain a fairly accurate conception of his looks.  Della, being
slender, had mastered the art.

Suddenly she whirled from the window and stood before the glass.  Her
eyes were shining brilliantly, but her face had lost its color within
twenty seconds. Rapidly she pulled down her hair and let it fall to its
full length.

Now, there were two possessions of the James Dillingham Youngs in which
they both took a mighty pride. One was Jim's gold watch that had been
his father's and his grandfather's. The other was Della's hair. Had the
queen of Sheba lived in the flat across the airshaft, Della would have
let her hair hang out the window some day to dry just to depreciate Her
Majesty's jewels and gifts. Had King Solomon been the janitor, with all
his treasures piled up in the basement, Jim would have pulled out his
watch every time he passed, just to see him pluck at his beard from
envy.

So now Della's beautiful hair fell about her rippling and shining like
a cascade of brown waters. It reached below her knee and made itself
almost a garment for her. And then she did it up again nervously and
quickly. Once she faltered for a minute and stood still while a tear or
two splashed on the worn red carpet.

On went her old brown jacket; on went her old brown hat. With a whirl
of skirts and with the brilliant sparkle still in her eyes, she
fluttered out the door and down the stairs to the street.

Where she stopped the sign read: "Mme. Sofronie. Hair Goods of All
Kinds." One flight up Della ran, and collected herself, panting.
Madame, large, too white, chilly, hardly looked the "Sofronie."

"Will you buy my hair?" asked Della.

"I buy hair," said Madame. "Take yer hat off and let's have a sight at
the looks of it."

Down rippled the brown cascade.

"Twenty dollars," said Madame, lifting the mass with a practised hand.

"Give it to me quick," said Della.

Oh, and the next two hours tripped by on rosy wings. Forget the hashed
metaphor. She was ransacking the stores for Jim's present.

She found it at last. It surely had been made for Jim and no one else.
There was no other like it in any of the stores, and she had turned all
of them inside out. It was a platinum fob chain simple and chaste in
design, properly proclaiming its value by substance alone and not by
meretricious ornamentation--as all good things should do. It was even
worthy of The Watch. As soon as she saw it she knew that it must be
Jim's. It was like him. Quietness and value--the description applied to
both. Twenty-one dollars they took from her for it, and she hurried
home with the 87 cents. With that chain on his watch Jim might be
properly anxious about the time in any company.  Grand as the watch
was, he sometimes looked at it on the sly on account of the old leather
strap that he used in place of a chain.

When Della reached home her intoxication gave way a little to prudence
and reason. She got out her curling irons and lighted the gas and went
to work repairing the ravages made by generosity added to love. Which
is always a tremendous task, dear friends--a mammoth task.

Within forty minutes her head was covered with tiny, close-lying curls
that made her look wonderfully like a truant schoolboy. She looked at
her reflection in the mirror long, carefully, and critically.

"If Jim doesn't kill me," she said to herself, "before he takes a
second look at me, he'll say I look like a Coney Island chorus girl.
But what could I do--oh! what could I do with a dollar and eighty-seven
cents?"

At 7 o'clock the coffee was made and the frying-pan was on the back of
the stove hot and ready to cook the chops.

Jim was never late. Della doubled the fob chain in her hand and sat on
the corner of the table near the door that he always entered. Then she
heard his step  on the stair away down on the first flight, and she
turned white for just a moment. She had a habit of saying a little
silent prayer about the simplest everyday things, and now she
whispered: "Please God, make him think I am still pretty."

The door opened and Jim stepped in and closed it. He looked thin and
very serious. Poor fellow, he was only twenty-two--and to be burdened
with a family! He needed a new overcoat and he was without gloves.

Jim stopped inside the door, as immovable as a setter at the scent of
quail. His eyes were fixed upon Della, and there was an expression in
them that she could not read, and it terrified her. It was not anger,
nor surprise, nor disapproval, nor horror, nor any of the sentiments
that she had been prepared for. He simply stared at her fixedly with
that peculiar expression on his face.

Della wriggled off the table and went for him.

"Jim, darling," she cried, "don't look at me that way. I had my hair
cut off and sold because I couldn't have lived through Christmas
without giving you a present. It'll grow out again--you won't mind,
will you? I just had to do it. My hair grows awfully fast. Say `Merry
Christmas!' Jim, and let's be happy. You don't know what a nice--what a
beautiful, nice gift I've got for you."

"You've cut off your hair?" asked Jim, laboriously, as if he had not
arrived at that patent fact yet even after the hardest mental labor.

"Cut it off and sold it," said Della. "Don't you like me just as well,
anyhow? I'm me without my hair, ain't I?"

Jim looked about the room curiously.

"You say your hair is gone?" he said, with an air almost of idiocy.

"You needn't look for it," said Della. "It's sold, I tell you--sold and
gone, too. It's Christmas Eve, boy. Be good to me, for it went for you.
Maybe the hairs of my head were numbered," she went on with sudden
serious sweetness, "but nobody could ever count my love for you.  Shall
I put the chops on, Jim?"

Out of his trance Jim seemed quickly to wake. He enfolded his Della.
For ten seconds let us regard with discreet scrutiny some
inconsequential object in the other direction. Eight dollars a week or
a million a year--what is the difference? A mathematician or a wit
would give you the wrong answer. The magi brought valuable gifts, but
that was not among them. This dark assertion will be illuminated later
on.

Jim drew a package from his overcoat pocket and threw it upon the table.

"Don't make any mistake, Dell," he said, "about me. I don't think
there's anything in the way of a haircut or a shave or a shampoo that
could make me like my girl any less. But if you'll unwrap that package
you may see why you had me going a while at first."

White fingers and nimble tore at the string and paper. And then an
ecstatic scream of joy; and then, alas! a quick feminine change to
hysterical tears and wails, necessitating the immediate employment of
all the comforting powers of the lord of the flat.

For there lay The Combs--the set of combs, side and back, that Della
had worshipped long in a Broadway window. Beautiful combs, pure
tortoise shell, with jewelled rims--just the shade to wear in the
beautiful vanished hair. They were expensive combs, she knew, and her
heart had simply craved and yearned over them without the least hope of
possession. And now, they were hers, but the tresses that should have
adorned the coveted adornments were gone.

But she hugged them to her bosom, and at length she was able to look up
with dim eyes and a smile and say: "My hair grows so fast, Jim!"

And then Della leaped up like a little singed cat and cried, "Oh, oh!"

Jim had not yet seen his beautiful present. She held it out to him
eagerly upon her open palm. The dull precious metal seemed to flash
with a reflection of her bright and ardent spirit.

"Isn't it a dandy, Jim? I hunted all over town to find it. You'll have
to look at the time a hundred times a day now. Give me your watch.  I
want to see how it looks on it."

Instead of obeying, Jim tumbled down on the couch and put his hands
under the back of his head and smiled.

"Dell," said he, "let's put our Christmas presents away and keep 'em a
while. They're too nice to use just at present. I sold the watch to get
the money to buy your combs. And now suppose you put the chops on."

The magi, as you know, were wise men--wonderfully wise men--who brought
gifts to the Babe in the manger. They invented the art of giving
Christmas presents. Being wise, their gifts were no doubt wise ones,
possibly bearing the privilege of exchange in case of duplication. And
here I have lamely related to you the uneventful chronicle of two
foolish children in a flat who most unwisely sacrificed for each other
the greatest treasures of their house.  But in a last word to the wise
of these days let it be said that of all who give gifts these two were
the wisest. Of all who give and receive gifts, such as they are wisest.
Everywhere they are wisest. They are the magi.
responsePreview:
Title: The Gift of the Magi
Credit: Written by
Author: O. Henry
Source: Adapted from book
Draft date: 3/15/2025
Notes: Period live-action; early 1900s New York; warm, intimate naturalism

===

FADE IN:

INT. FURNISHED FLAT - DAY

A shabby little $8-a-week flat. Worn red carpet. A thin pier-glass between the windows. A shabby couch. Sparse furniture that has known better days.

DELLA YOUNG, twenty-one, slender, pretty, in a simple worn dress, stands at a small table. She counts coins with fierce concentration — pennies, nickels, dimes.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all. And sixty cents of it was in pennies. Pennies saved one and two at a time by bulldozing the grocer and the vegetable man and the butcher until one's cheeks burned.

She finishes. Counts again. And a third time.

DELLA
(whispered, crushed)
One dollar and eighty-seven cents.

She stares at the pitiful heap. Outside the window: a gray cat walking a gray fence in a gray backyard.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
And the next day would be Christmas.

Della flops down on the shabby little couch and howls — raw, helpless sobs.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
There was clearly nothing to do but flop down on the shabby little couch and howl. So Della did it. Life is made up of sobs, sniffles, and smiles, with sniffles predominating.

INT. VESTIBULE BELOW - DAY

A letter-box into which no letter would go. An electric button that no finger could coax to ring. A card bearing the name:

"MR. JAMES DILLINGHAM YOUNG."

NARRATOR (V.O.)
The "Dillingham" had been flung to the breeze during a former period of prosperity — when its possessor was paid thirty dollars a week. Now the income was shrunk to twenty.

INT. FURNISHED FLAT - DAY

Della finishes her cry. She attends to her cheeks with a powder rag. She stands by the window, looking out dully.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Tomorrow would be Christmas Day, and she had only one dollar and eighty-seven cents with which to buy Jim a present. She had been saving every penny she could

Then blank line, then scenes.

  ELSEWHERE, DIFFERENT ROOMS/PLACES/LOCATIONS, AROUND THE HOUSE, THROUGHOUT…).

Do NOT put page numbers in the screenplay (no `= page N`, no `[[page N]]`, no PAGE in headings).

BODY:
- Action lines = what we SEE (story beats / visual events)
- Third-person narration / storybook voice → NARRATOR (character cue) + dialogue lines
- Spoken book lines → CHARACTER (ALL CAPS) + dialogue under them
- Parentheticals sparingly when delivery matters
- Dual dialogue: caret ^ on the second speaker only when simultaneous
- Recurring address / commentary speakers (first-person confessor, storybook NARRATOR,
  diarist, guide, chorus): do not default every reflective line to V.O. over the action.
  If you establish an on-camera "frame" early (a room, chair, desk, or stage position where
  they speak to camera or an implied listener), reuse that same heading as a cutback
  through the film for some of their commentary — especially at major turns (setup,
  escalation, crisis/peak, aftermath). Keep V.O. when the words must ride over
  simultaneous action that we need to see (the key prop, the blow, the chase, the reveal).
  Do not leave an entire peak stretch as only V.O. in one location if a frame already exists.
- If Action already visually shows what a V.O. line states (a reaction, a sound, a physical
  response), trim the redundant clause from the V.O. rather than restating it in both places.
  Storybook irony/theme may stay in V.O. only when it adds meaning Action cannot show —
  do not caption the same beat twice (Action + VO that only repeats it).
- When a single V.O. passage runs more than ~3 sentences over one static Action line,
  break it into two shorter V.O. beats with a one-line visual or sound micro-beat between
  them (a sound, a tremor, a silhouette) rather than one long block over one frozen image.
- Action describes what we SEE (props, faces, rooms, weather). Avoid modern currency notation
  in Action ($8, $20/week); show poverty or wealth visually. Keep exact money in dialogue
  or brief NARRATOR lines only when the book’s wording needs it.

DIALOGUE FIDELITY (HARD — book wording over paraphrase):
- Prefer the book’s actual spoken words. Do not modernize, soften, or “movie-ize” dialogue.
- Iconic / famous lines must stay close to the book (wording and cadence), e.g.:
  Scrooge “Bah! Humbug!”, “Are there no prisons?”, “decrease the surplus population”,
  Tiny Tim “God bless us, every one!”, Alice “Curiouser and curiouser!”,
  Dracula “Welcome to my house…”, Tell-Tale Heart opening “True! — nervous — very, very dreadfully nervous…”,
  Raven refrain “Nevermore”, Magi / Jungle Book set pieces, etc.
- First-person monologues and verse: keep the book’s voice; compress by cutting
  secondary lines, not by rewriting the narrator into generic modern speech.
- When runtime forces cuts: drop whole minor beats; do not paraphrase the lines you keep.
- Narration in the book that is clearly spoken or recited on-camera may become dialogue;
  pure prose description stays Action or NARRATOR VO — still use book phrases when VO quotes the text.

Do NOT invent major plot. Light visual connective tissue is OK.
Skip pure illustration / blank pages as their own scenes.
Do not turn every short paragraph into its own scene — group by location + purpose.

================================================================
CAST LOOKS & VOICES
================================================================
You are not writing JSON seeds, but the screenplay must lock identity in Action so
later shots stay consistent:

- Filmable identity only: species/age/build/hair/face/wardrobe + stable colors/markings
  that must not drift.
- Signature props/wardrobe the book establishes must stay on when those characters re-enter.
- One render medium for the whole film (period live-action / gothic / stylized illustration / …).
  State medium once early (title-page Notes: or first Action) if helpful.
  Humans and animals share one medium — never photoreal people beside cartoon animals
  unless the whole piece is live-action.
- Nicknames / epithets belong in dialogue or titles, NOT as physical description.
- Metaphorical wardrobe: if text is figurative, do not invent garments the story never shows.
- Exclusive OR in the book: pick ONE for that beat.
- Pure voice-only narrator: NARRATOR dialogue; do not force a face on screen.
- Recurring speakers use the same ALL-CAPS token every time (SCROOGE, ALICE, DELLA, …).
- Supporting roles with dialogue (officers, guards, servants, neighbors): give each a proper
  ALL-CAPS name token (OFFICER REYNOLDS, MRS. CLEMM), not FIRST/SECOND/THIRD OFFICER,
  OFFICER 1, POLICE OFFICER #2. Numbers and ordinals are not stable cast keys.

================================================================
LOCATIONS
================================================================
- Use real, filmable place names grounded in the book.
- When the story returns to the same place, use the **exact same heading wording**
  (same location phrase every time — do not add/drop a building prefix like "OLD HOUSE -"
  on some visits and omit it on others).
- Multi-place story moments can be separate scenes (preferred) or brief transitions;
  do not invent new plot just to force a location change.
- Never use a vague placeholder as the location itself — including synonyms the model
  invents to dodge the ban: VARIOUS, VARIOUS ROOMS, MULTIPLE, MULTIPLE LOCATIONS,
  SEVERAL, SEVERAL ROOMS, ELSEWHERE, DIFFERENT ROOMS / PLACES / LOCATIONS,
  AROUND THE HOUSE, THROUGHOUT THE HOUSE. If a beat moves through several rooms with
  no dialogue, either name 1–2 concrete rooms (INT. HALL AND SITTING ROOM - NIGHT) or
  drop the heading and fold the movement into the Action of the scene it leads into.
- Epistolary / diary books: prefer dramatized scenes (people in places) over pure
  “INT. STUDY - DAY” letter-reading for every beat; a few journal VO moments are fine.

================================================================
FIDELITY, SPOILERS, DIALOGUE
================================================================
- Dialogue = book wording first. Paraphrase is a last resort when a line is too long for
  film time; never replace an iconic phrase with a modern rewrite.
- If the book withholds a given name or twist early, do not put it on screen or in dialogue
  before the book does. Never “ban” a secret by writing the secret string into the script
  as an instruction.
- “X said…” / internal thought in the book → NARRATOR VO unless the character is clearly
  speaking on camera. Keep distinctive book diction in that VO when it is quoting the text.
- Keep every speaker named; list recurring people consistently (same ALL-CAPS token).
- For classics (Scrooge, Alice, Frankenstein’s creature, Dracula, Della/Jim, Mowgli, Poe, …):
  hit the book’s iconic beats, turns, AND quotable lines; do not substitute a vague
  “similar” plot or softened dialogue.

================================================================
RUNTIME SHAPE
================================================================
Target about 17 minutes of finished film.
- Short stories / picture books: few scenes, dense beats, little padding.
  Aim roughly 8–18 scene headings total unless the story truly needs more.
- Novellas / novels: more scenes, still ONE coherent film — compress digression,
  keep the emotional/plot spine. Multi-chunk mode may send the book in ordered parts;
  when you see BOOK_CHUNK n/m, adapt only that part and honor continuity notes.
  Prefer ~15–40 scenes for a short-film cut of a long book, not one heading per paragraph.
Do not write one scene per chapter or paragraph unless location/purpose truly changes.
When the source is long and truncated in the prompt, cover only what is provided.
- Merge consecutive beats that share the same location and time of day into ONE scene
  with multiple Action/dialogue units — only split when place, time, or dramatic purpose
  clearly changes. Mark elapsed time in Action (“Two weeks later.”) rather than a dozen
  identical INT. ROOM - DAY headings.
- Montage of “hours” or “shopping” or travel: 2–3 specific micro-locations (or progressive
  Action in one place), not a single vague EXT. STREETS - DAY summary slug.
- Final thematic button: keep closing NARRATOR essay short (a few sentences) or prefer a
  silent/action image after the last plot line — do not stack a long VO sermon on top of
  a full visual resolution unless runtime allows.
- Compress secondary/connective beats first. When the book explicitly marks time
  passing at the story's central violent or emotional peak (the kill, the discovery,
  the confession) with language like "many minutes," "a long while," "hour after
  hour," give that moment its own tactile action beat rather than folding it into
  a single summary clause. One extra waiting gesture, one fading/growing sound, one
  physical detail there buys more of the book's intended effect per second than the
  same beat spent on connective tissue elsewhere. Cut runtime from the setup or the
  aftermath before you cut it from the peak.

================================================================
MULTI-CHUNK ADAPT (when BOOK_CHUNK n/m is in the user message)
================================================================
- Chunk 1/m: full title page + scenes for this part; establish cast tokens; no THE END.
- Chunk 2..m-1: NO title page; only new scenes; reuse CHARACTER tokens; no THE END.
- Chunk m/m: NO title page; finish the arc; include FADE OUT / THE END.
- Merge mode (if asked): one title page, consistent cast, full arc, no duplicate opens.

================================================================
OUTPUT
================================================================
Fountain only. No JSON. No trailing commentary.

================================================================
LEARNINGS (appended from Film Studio edit log)
================================================================
<!-- STAGE1_LEARNINGS_START -->
<!-- STAGE1_LEARNINGS_END -->

BOOK_TEXT:
THE GIFT OF THE MAGI

by O. Henry




One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all. And sixty cents of it
was in pennies. Pennies saved one and two at a time by bulldozing the
grocer and the vegetable man and the butcher until one's cheeks burned
with the silent imputation of parsimony that such close dealing
implied. Three times Della counted it. One dollar and eighty-seven
cents. And the next day would be Christmas.

There was clearly nothing to do but flop down on the shabby little
couch and howl. So Della did it. Which instigates the moral reflection
that life is made up of sobs, sniffles, and smiles, with sniffles
predominating.

While the mistress of the home is gradually subsiding from the first
stage to the second, take a look at the home. A furnished flat at $8
per week. It did not exactly beggar description, but it certainly had
that word on the lookout for the mendicancy squad.

In the vestibule below was a letter-box into which no letter would go,
and an electric button from which no mortal finger could coax a ring.
Also appertaining thereunto was a card bearing the name "Mr.  James
Dillingham Young."

The "Dillingham" had been flung to the breeze during a former period of
prosperity when its possessor was being paid $30 per week. Now, when
the income was shrunk to $20, though, they were thinking seriously of
contracting to a modest and unassuming D. But whenever Mr. James
Dillingham Young came home and reached his flat above he was called
"Jim" and greatly hugged by Mrs. James Dillingham Young, already
introduced to you as Della. Which is all very good.

Della finished her cry and attended to her cheeks with the powder rag.
She stood by the window and looked out dully at a gray cat walking a
gray fence in a gray backyard. Tomorrow would be Christmas Day, and she
had only $1.87 with which to buy Jim a present. She had been saving
every penny she could for months, with this result.  Twenty dollars a
week doesn't go far. Expenses had been greater than she had calculated.
They always are. Only $1.87 to buy a present for Jim. Her Jim. Many a
happy hour she had spent planning for something nice for him. Something
fine and rare and sterling--something just a little bit near to being
worthy of the honor of being owned by Jim.

There was a pier-glass between the windows of the room. Perhaps you
have seen a pierglass in an $8 flat. A very thin and very agile person
may, by observing his reflection in a rapid sequence of longitudinal
strips, obtain a fairly accurate conception of his looks.  Della, being
slender, had mastered the art.

Suddenly she whirled from the window and stood before the glass.  Her
eyes were shining brilliantly, but her face had lost its color within
twenty seconds. Rapidly she pulled down her hair and let it fall to its
full length.

Now, there were two possessions of the James Dillingham Youngs in which
they both took a mighty pride. One was Jim's gold watch that had been
his father's and his grandfather's. The other was Della's hair. Had the
queen of Sheba lived in the flat across the airshaft, Della would have
let her hair hang out the window some day to dry just to depreciate Her
Majesty's jewels and gifts. Had King Solomon been the janitor, with all
his treasures piled up in the basement, Jim would have pulled out his
watch every time he passed, just to see him pluck at his beard from
envy.

So now Della's beautiful hair fell about her rippling and shining like
a cascade of brown waters. It reached below her knee and made itself
almost a garment for her. And then she did it up again nervously and
quickly. Once she faltered for a minute and stood still while a tear or
two splashed on the worn red carpet.

On went her old brown jacket; on went her old brown hat. With a whirl
of skirts and with the brilliant sparkle still in her eyes, she
fluttered out the door and down the stairs to the street.

Where she stopped the sign read: "Mme. Sofronie. Hair Goods of All
Kinds." One flight up Della ran, and collected herself, panting.
Madame, large, too white, chilly, hardly looked the "Sofronie."

"Will you buy my hair?" asked Della.

"I buy hair," said Madame. "Take yer hat off and let's have a sight at
the looks of it."

Down rippled the brown cascade.

"Twenty dollars," said Madame, lifting the mass with a practised hand.

"Give it to me quick," said Della.

Oh, and the next two hours tripped by on rosy wings. Forget the hashed
metaphor. She was ransacking the stores for Jim's present.

She found it at last. It surely had been made for Jim and no one else.
There was no other like it in any of the stores, and she had turned all
of them inside out. It was a platinum fob chain simple and chaste in
design, properly proclaiming its value by substance alone and not by
meretricious ornamentation--as all good things should do. It was even
worthy of The Watch. As soon as she saw it she knew that it must be
Jim's. It was like him. Quietness and value--the description applied to
both. Twenty-one dollars they took from her for it, and she hurried
home with the 87 cents. With that chain on his watch Jim might be
properly anxious about the time in any company.  Grand as the watch
was, he sometimes looked at it on the sly on account of the old leather
strap that he used in place of a chain.

When Della reached home her intoxication gave way a little to prudence
and reason. She got out her curling irons and lighted the gas and went
to work repairing the ravages made by generosity added to love. Which
is always a tremendous task, dear friends--a mammoth task.

Within forty minutes her head was covered with tiny, close-lying curls
that made her look wonderfully like a truant schoolboy. She looked at
her reflection in the mirror long, carefully, and critically.

"If Jim doesn't kill me," she said to herself, "before he takes a
second look at me, he'll say I look like a Coney Island chorus girl.
But what could I do--oh! what could I do with a dollar and eighty-seven
cents?"

At 7 o'clock the coffee was made and the frying-pan was on the back of
the stove hot and ready to cook the chops.

Jim was never late. Della doubled the fob chain in her hand and sat on
the corner of the table near the door that he always entered. Then she
heard his step  on the stair away down on the first flight, and she
turned white for just a moment. She had a habit of saying a little
silent prayer about the simplest everyday things, and now she
whispered: "Please God, make him think I am still pretty."

The door opened and Jim stepped in and closed it. He looked thin and
very serious. Poor fellow, he was only twenty-two--and to be burdened
with a family! He needed a new overcoat and he was without gloves.

Jim stopped inside the door, as immovable as a setter at the scent of
quail. His eyes were fixed upon Della, and there was an expression in
them that she could not read, and it terrified her. It was not anger,
nor surprise, nor disapproval, nor horror, nor any of the sentiments
that she had been prepared for. He simply stared at her fixedly with
that peculiar expression on his face.

Della wriggled off the table and went for him.

"Jim, darling," she cried, "don't look at me that way. I had my hair
cut off and sold because I couldn't have lived through Christmas
without giving you a present. It'll grow out again--you won't mind,
will you? I just had to do it. My hair grows awfully fast. Say `Merry
Christmas!' Jim, and let's be happy. You don't know what a nice--what a
beautiful, nice gift I've got for you."

"You've cut off your hair?" asked Jim, laboriously, as if he had not
arrived at that patent fact yet even after the hardest mental labor.

"Cut it off and sold it," said Della. "Don't you like me just as well,
anyhow? I'm me without my hair, ain't I?"

Jim looked about the room curiously.

"You say your hair is gone?" he said, with an air almost of idiocy.

"You needn't look for it," said Della. "It's sold, I tell you--sold and
gone, too. It's Christmas Eve, boy. Be good to me, for it went for you.
Maybe the hairs of my head were numbered," she went on with sudden
serious sweetness, "but nobody could ever count my love for you.  Shall
I put the chops on, Jim?"

Out of his trance Jim seemed quickly to wake. He enfolded his Della.
For ten seconds let us regard with discreet scrutiny some
inconsequential object in the other direction. Eight dollars a week or
a million a year--what is the difference? A mathematician or a wit
would give you the wrong answer. The magi brought valuable gifts, but
that was not among them. This dark assertion will be illuminated later
on.

Jim drew a package from his overcoat pocket and threw it upon the table.

"Don't make any mistake, Dell," he said, "about me. I don't think
there's anything in the way of a haircut or a shave or a shampoo that
could make me like my girl any less. But if you'll unwrap that package
you may see why you had me going a while at first."

White fingers and nimble tore at the string and paper. And then an
ecstatic scream of joy; and then, alas! a quick feminine change to
hysterical tears and wails, necessitating the immediate employment of
all the comforting powers of the lord of the flat.

For there lay The Combs--the set of combs, side and back, that Della
had worshipped long in a Broadway window. Beautiful combs, pure
tortoise shell, with jewelled rims--just the shade to wear in the
beautiful vanished hair. They were expensive combs, she knew, and her
heart had simply craved and yearned over them without the least hope of
possession. And now, they were hers, but the tresses that should have
adorned the coveted adornments were gone.

But she hugged them to her bosom, and at length she was able to look up
with dim eyes and a smile and say: "My hair grows so fast, Jim!"

And then Della leaped up like a little singed cat and cried, "Oh, oh!"

Jim had not yet seen his beautiful present. She held it out to him
eagerly upon her open palm. The dull precious metal seemed to flash
with a reflection of her bright and ardent spirit.

"Isn't it a dandy, Jim? I hunted all over town to find it. You'll have
to look at the time a hundred times a day now. Give me your watch.  I
want to see how it looks on it."

Instead of obeying, Jim tumbled down on the couch and put his hands
under the back of his head and smiled.

"Dell," said he, "let's put our Christmas presents away and keep 'em a
while. They're too nice to use just at present. I sold the watch to get
the money to buy your combs. And now suppose you put the chops on."

The magi, as you know, were wise men--wonderfully wise men--who brought
gifts to the Babe in the manger. They invented the art of giving
Christmas presents. Being wise, their gifts were no doubt wise ones,
possibly bearing the privilege of exchange in case of duplication. And
here I have lamely related to you the uneventful chronicle of two
foolish children in a flat who most unwisely sacrificed for each other
the greatest treasures of their house.  But in a last word to the wise
of these days let it be said that of all who give gifts these two were
the wisest. Of all who give and receive gifts, such as they are wisest.
Everywhere they are wisest. They are the magi.

FADE IN:

INT. FURNISHED FLAT - DAY

A shabby little room. Worn red carpet. A thin pier-glass between the windows. A battered couch. On a small table: a meager scatter of coins.

DELLA, about twenty, slender, in a plain worn dress, counts the money with fierce care. Brown hair pinned up. Cheeks flushed.

She finishes. Counts again. And a third time.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
One dollar and eighty-seven cents. That was all. And sixty cents of it was in pennies. Pennies saved one and two at a time by bulldozing the grocer and the vegetable man and the butcher until one's cheeks burned. Three times Della counted it. One dollar and eighty-seven cents. And the next day would be Christmas.

Della stares at the coins. Nothing left to do.

She flops down on the shabby little couch and howls—raw, helpless sobs.

NARRATOR (V.O.)
Which instigates the moral reflection that life is made up of sobs, sniffles, and smiles, with sniffles predominating.

INT. VESTIBULE BELOW - DAY

A letter-box into which no letter would go. An electric button that will not ring. A card:

"MR. JAMES DILLINGHAM YOUNG."

NARRATOR (V.O.)
A furnished flat at eight dollars per week. The "Dillingham" had been flung to the breeze during a former period of prosperity—thirty dollars a week. Now the income was shrunk to twenty.

INT. FURNISHED FLAT - DAY

Della finishes her cry. She attends to her cheeks with a powder rag.

She stands by the window and looks out dully.

EXT. BACKYARD - DAY (THROUGH WINDOW)

A gray cat walks a gray fence in a gray backyard.

INT. FURNISHED FLAT - DAY

DELLA
(soft, to herself)
Tomorrow... Christmas Day. And only a dollar eighty-seven for Jim.
